elements.sent-2411-001: Tropical convection plays an important role in shaping the tropical climate \citep{emanuel1994book .}
elements.sent-2411-002: There are generally two perspectives to studying tropical convection.
elements.sent-2411-003: One perspective is from the microscopic level and aims to quantify the lifecycle of individual clouds \cite[e.g.,][]{byers1949thunderstorm,ogura1971numerical,ferrier1989one,morrison2020part1 .}
elements.sent-2411-004: The other perspective is from the macroscopic level, which studies the statistical behavior of many clouds, i.e., a cloud ensemble \citep{arakawa1974interaction .}
elements.sent-2411-005: The cloud ensemble might be understood as a medium-size cloud population, which is large enough to yield a statistical equilibrium state and small enough to avoid background gradient in their properties.
elements.sent-2411-006: An open question is how the cloud ensemble responds to changes in external forcing \citep{betts1986new,tompkins1998time,cohen2004response,davies2009memory .}
elements.sent-2411-007: They revealed two adjustment timescales: a fast $O(1)$ hour timescale for gravity wave propagating a mean cloud spacing and a slow $O(10)$ days timescale for moist static energy to be vertically transported across the troposphere.
elements.sent-2411-008: When the forcing period is too slow (36 hours), convection is in quasi-equilibrium with the forcing; when it is too short (1 hour), convection hardly feels the forcing; and when it is 3-12 hours, the convective amplitude varies from cycle to cycle as if there is a wave packet.
elements.sent-2411-009: It seems necessary to include microscopic processes in understanding the response property of a cloud ensemble \cite[e.g.,][]{khouider2003coarse,colin2021atmospheric,yano2023interaction .}
elements.sent-2411-010: Much effort has been devoted to modeling the prognostic property of a cloud ensemble, the basis for understanding its response property.
elements.sent-2411-011: A crucial yet semi-empirical closure is a square-law relation between the convective kinetic energy and the cloud-base mass flux.
elements.sent-2411-012: Other studies have interpreted the dynamics of a cloud ensemble in different contexts with the broadly defined predator-prey model \citep{koren2011prey,koren2017chaos_linearized,pujol2019limit_cycle,colin2021atmospheric,yang2024predator .}
elements.sent-2411-013: It remains unclear how a cloud ensemble's lifecycle fundamentally differs from an individual cloud's, causing puzzles in designing convective parameterization schemes \citep{emanuel1994large,mapes1997review .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Our answer is that a cloud ensemble has many clouds that might be at different lifecycle stages.
elements.sent-2411-015: This motivates us to study the phase of a convective lifecycle and how it responds to external forcing.
elements.sent-2411-016: When all members of a cloud ensemble initiate and dissipate simultaneously, we call it a ``synchronized state".
elements.sent-2411-017: The only paper we know that focuses on cloud synchronization is by \citet{feingold2013coupled .}
elements.sent-2411-018: They devised a predator-prey type coupled oscillator model for mesoscale cellular convection, a shallow convective system frequently seen over subtropical oceans \citep{agee1978structure .}
elements.sent-2411-019: The governing variables are the cloud top height, droplet concentration, and rainfall rate.
elements.sent-2411-020: The neighboring clouds are assumed to couple via the downdraft-driven boundary layer flow, which is parameterized as a delayed function of the neighboring cloud's height growth rate.
elements.sent-2411-021: The cloud ensemble self-synchronizes when the coupling strength parameter takes a moderate value.
elements.sent-2411-022: This model impressively demonstrates individual convection's phase, period, and amplitude adjustment.
elements.sent-2411-023: It carries many regimes that await further study.
elements.sent-2411-024: However, it is probably too complicated to yield an analytical solution, preventing further interpretation of physics.
elements.sent-2411-025: The physical basis of this model involves some empirical considerations to fit it into the classic predator-prey type model.
elements.sent-2411-026: Whether this model can be applied to tropical convection also remains unclear.
elements.sent-2411-027: Thus, we decided to devise a simpler model focusing on the convective lifecycle phase.
elements.sent-2411-028: It should analytically reveal the statistical behavior of synchronization and how synchronization could bridge the microscopic and macroscopic cloud dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-029: We also need an objective index to quantify synchronization in observational data, cloud-permitting simulations, or simple models.
elements.sent-2411-030: This paper is organized in the following way.
elements.sent-2411-031: In section \ref{sec:RCE , we introduce an example problem, the convectively coupled gravity waves (CCGWs) simulated in a finite domain, for understanding the response of a cloud ensemble to periodic external forcing.}
elements.sent-2411-032: Multiscale analysis shows that the growth of CCGW is significantly contributed by the synchronization of convective lifecycles, which can be quantified with an index.
elements.sent-2411-033: In section \ref{sec:micro_model , we propose a simple microscopic model of the convective lifecycle to reproduce the synchronization behavior.}
elements.sent-2411-034: The microscopic model has a fixed amplitude, but the wave-induced large-scale vertical motion can adjust its phase.
elements.sent-2411-035: An analytical solution of the model shows that the phase adjustment of individual clouds makes the cloud ensemble an approximate harmonic oscillator.
elements.sent-2411-036: Section \ref{sec:discussion discusses the limitations of the simple model, followed by a conclusion in section \ref{sec:conclusion .}}
elements.section-2411-002: Evidence of synchronization in an RCE simulation
elements.sent-2411-037: We simulate CCGW in an idealized configuration, the radiative-convective equilibrium (RCE) setup.
elements.sent-2411-038: It is a simulation configuration with no Coriolis force, no initial background wind, a uniform sea surface temperature, and periodic boundary conditions in the two horizontal directions.
elements.sent-2411-039: The RCE setup is widely used in studying the statistical behavior of tropical convection \cite[e.g.,][]{held1993radiative,tompkins2001coldpool,bretherton2005energy,romps2014analytical,wing2018convective .}
elements.sent-2411-040: CCGWs have been reported in many RCE simulations, with or without Coriolis force \citep{bretherton2005energy,bretherton2006interpretation,nolan2007trigger,tulich2007vertical,o2017accessible,yang2019convective,yang2020interactive,fu2023small .}
elements.sent-2411-041: The waves are usually in a standing pattern caused by the interference of counter propagating waves.
elements.sent-2411-042: Their wavelength is severely constrained by the domain size, if not wide enough.
elements.sent-2411-043: As a result, the standing wave has been attributed to be largely irrelevant to the real atmosphere.
elements.sent-2411-044: We argue that this limitation can be useful for specific problems, such as studying the response of a cloud ensemble to waves with controllable wavelength and frequency.
elements.sent-2411-045: We will report the convective synchronization phenomenon in a finite-domain RCE simulation, with an alert that the wave could be different from CCGWs in the real atmosphere.
elements.sent-2411-046: We use Cloud Model 1 \cite[CM1, version 19.10,][]{bryan2002benchmark to perform a cloud-permitting RCE simulation in a 1080 km by 1080 km doubly periodic square domain with a fixed sea surface temperature of 300 K.}
elements.sent-2411-047: The setup is identical to that used by \citet{fu2023small except for setting zero Coriolis parameter here.}
elements.sent-2411-048: The domain height is 27 km, with a sponge layer above 20 km to attenuate gravity waves.
elements.sent-2411-049: The mesh is vertically nonuniform, with eight mesh layers in the lowest 1 km to resolve the boundary layer processes.
elements.sent-2411-050: The physics parameterization package includes the Morrison double-moment microphysics scheme \citep{morrison2005new , the RRTMG radiative transfer scheme \citep{clough2005RRTM , the surface model based on the similarity theory \citep{jimenez2012revised , and the simple boundary layer scheme from \citet{bryan2009maximum .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-051: To remove the influence of the diurnal cycle, the solar zenith angle is set a constant.
elements.sent-2411-052: The simulation is initiated with an RCE-state sounding plus random noise of potential temperature in the lowest five mesh layers and runs for 10 days.
elements.sent-2411-053: The data output frequency is every 0.5 hours.
elements.sent-2411-054: The RCE-state sounding is the last day output of a 100-day small-domain simulation with a horizontal domain size of 120 km $\times$ 120 km.
elements.sent-2411-055: Next, we analyze the simulation results from a macroscopic and microscopic perspective and try reconciling them.

elements.sent-2411-074: Figure \ref{fig:2Dxz_all b shows the lower-tropospheric vertical velocity leads the surface rainfall rate by $\pi/2$ phase and is in phase with shallow convection.}
elements.sent-2411-075: Shallow convection may respond to boundary layer convergence almost instantaneously and be the preconditioner for deep convection, thereby leading precipitation.. \cite[e.g.,][]{khouider2006simple,liu2022convective .}
elements.sent-2411-076: Figure \ref{fig:2Dxz_all c shows that the horizontal anomaly of lower-tropospheric potential temperature $\widetilde{\theta}$ is in the opposite phase of the rainfall.}
elements.sent-2411-077: The second baroclinic mode dominates the vertical structure of $\widetilde{\theta}$ below 12 km height.\footnote{The vertical structure of $\widetilde{\theta}$ is different from $\widetilde{w}$.}

elements.sent-2411-079: The reason is that the first-mode wave has a faster intrinsic wave speed that spreads its temperature anomaly more smoothly in the domain than the second mode \citep{wu2003shallow .}
elements.sent-2411-080: Heavy precipitation, both due to deep convection and stratiform clouds, is in phase with a low-level cold anomaly, which reduces the low-level static stability and could invigorate convection.
elements.sent-2411-081: This indicates the presence of stratiform instability \citep{mapes2000toy,majda2001models,bretherton2005energy .}

elements.sent-2411-083: This agrees with the previous understanding that the transition from shallow to deep convection is accompanied by the gradual moistening of the lower troposphere \citep{khouider2006simple .}
elements.sent-2411-084: The above analysis shows that the standing wave in the RCE simulation is consistent with previous understandings of CCGW.
elements.sent-2411-085: This paper does not aim to model the wave growth rate.
elements.sent-2411-086: Instead, we use the CCGW as a lens to study the response of a cloud ensemble to large-scale vertical motion.
elements.sent-2411-087: The equivalent potential temperature $\theta_e$ is a bulk measure of humidity and buoyancy, a quantity closely related to the moist static energy (MSE) in the lower troposphere \citep{riehl1979climate .}
elements.sent-2411-088: The $\theta_e$ is a conserved variable in water phase change and a convenient tool for analyzing the convective lifecycle.
elements.sent-2411-089: Without considering the ice phase process, $\theta_e$ can be approximated as \cite[e.g.,][]{siebesma1998shallow : \begin{equation}\label{eq:thetae_simplified} \theta_e \approx \theta \exp \left( \frac{L_v q_v}{c_p T} \right),\end{equation} where $T$ is temperature, $L_v \approx 2.5\times10^6$ J kg$^{-1}$ is the specific latent heat of water, and $c_p \approx 1005$ J kg$^{-1}$ K$^{-1}$ is the isobaric specific heat of air.}
elements.sent-2411-090: Traditionally, the $\theta_e$ or MSE in the mixed layer, i.e., lowest 0.5 km, has been used to represent the potential for convective initiation \citep{mapes1993gregarious,raymond1995BLQE,colin2021atmospheric .}
elements.sent-2411-091: However, \citet{fuglestvedt2020cold showed that moistening of the upper boundary layer (0.8 km to 1 km height) is also crucial for deep convective initiation.}
elements.sent-2411-092: In our simulation, both $\widetilde{q_v}$ and $\widetilde{\theta_e}$ have a distinct anomaly between 0.5 km and 1.4 km height (Fig. \ref{fig:the_profile b), which overlaps with the region of high vertical gradient of the background $\theta_e$ (Fig. \ref{fig:the_profile a).}}
elements.sent-2411-093: This region is likely the entrainment zone, a transition layer between the mixed layer and the free troposphere \cite[e.g.,][]{stull2012introduction .}
elements.sent-2411-094: Thus, we focus on the $\theta_e$ anomaly averaged between $z=0$ km and $z=H_B=1.43$ km, the lowest ten mesh layers.
elements.sent-2411-095: It is the boundary layer in a generalized sense, which includes the mixed layer and the entrainment zone.
elements.sent-2411-096: We hypothesize that the vertically averaged $\theta_e$ in the boundary layer is a crucial indicator of convective initiation and termination.

elements.sent-2411-127: In the last section, we found that the growth of CCGWs in the finite-domain simulation is significantly contributed by the synchronization of convective lifecycles, and quantified it with a synchronization index.
elements.sent-2411-128: In this section, we build a simple model to understand the mechanism of convective synchronization under a prescribed wave forcing.
elements.sent-2411-129: The model setup and simulation results are introduced in sections \ref{sec:micro_model}\ref{subsec:derivation_micro and \ref{sec:micro_model}\ref{subsec:microscopic_result , and an approximate analytical solution is presented in section \ref{sec:micro_model}\ref{subsec:oscillator .}}}

elements.sent-2411-131: The first step is to derive the governing equation of $\Theta_n$, representing the horizontal anomaly of the BL-averaged $\theta_e$.

elements.sent-2411-146: Here comes our key assumption: The system alternates between shallow and deep convection stages.
elements.sent-2411-147: During the shallow convection stage, shallow cumulus and congestus clouds form, while in the deep convection stage, deep convective clouds develop, which can detrain and lead to the formation of stratiform clouds.
elements.sent-2411-149: The convection term is negative (see Appendix B) because convection brings the free tropospheric dry air down to the boundary layer.

elements.sent-2411-153: When deep convection occurs, it consumes the convective available potential energy (CAPE) efficiently, decreasing $\Theta_n$.
elements.sent-2411-154: When $\Theta_n$ drops to the lower threshold, by which CAPE is used up, deep convection can no longer be sustained, and the deep stage transitions to the shallow stage.

elements.sent-2411-269: This paper investigates the synchronization of convective lifecycles, a collective behavior of tropical convection that has not received much attention.
elements.sent-2411-270: First, we use a coarse-graining technique to diagnose convective synchronization in a cloud-permitting RCE simulation.
elements.sent-2411-271: The horizontal anomaly of the boundary layer equivalent potential temperature averaged in 20 km $\times$ 20 km coarse-grained cells ($\Theta_n$) is used to represent the lifecycle of the $n^{th}$ cell, with $n=1$, 2, 3, ...
elements.sent-2411-272: We devised an amplitude decomposition algorithm, which shows that the response of a cloud ensemble to gravity waves is contributed by both the amplitude growth of individual clouds and their synchronization.
elements.sent-2411-273: The latter can be quantified with a synchronization index $I_{syn}$.
elements.sent-2411-274: To understand the synchronization process, we build a simple microscopic model of individual clouds governed by the variable $\Theta_n$.
elements.sent-2411-275: The evolution of $\Theta_n$ is driven by convection, radiative cooling, and surface heating and is modulated by gravity waves.
elements.sent-2411-276: The radiation is assumed to be a constant cooling effect, and the surface flux is a constant heating effect.
elements.sent-2411-277: The convection term always reduces $\Theta_n$ but has different strengths at different stages.
elements.sent-2411-278: At the shallow stage, where most clouds are shallow cumuli and congestus, the convection term is set to be an insignificantly negative value, and $\Theta_n$ gradually rises.
elements.sent-2411-279: When $\Theta_n$ reaches the upper threshold, i.e., CIN is eliminated, the system transitions to the deep stage, where most clouds are deep convection and stratiform clouds.
elements.sent-2411-280: The convection term is set to be a more negative value, driving $\Theta_n$ to drop.
elements.sent-2411-281: When $\Theta_n$ drops to the lower threshold, i.e., CAPE is exhausted, deep convection cannot self-sustain, and the system transitions to the shallow stage.
elements.sent-2411-282: The wave, parameterized as a periodic forcing, modulates $\Theta_n$ via the vertical advection of the background equivalent potential temperature in the boundary layer.
elements.sent-2411-283: The wave can influence the arrival time of $\Theta_n$ to the transition thresholds and, therefore, modulate its phase.
elements.sent-2411-284: The clouds in the microscopic model show an intriguing collective behavior.
elements.sent-2411-285: When the convective period equals the wave period, members in the cloud ensemble are gradually synchronized.
elements.sent-2411-286: The synchronization is explained as a phase adjustment process.
elements.sent-2411-287: For example, when convection lags the wave, the wave accelerates convection to make it catch the wave, adjusting it to a locked state where $\Theta_n$ is in phase with the wave vertical velocity $\widetilde{W}$.
elements.sent-2411-288: A quantitative analysis of the phase adjustment is performed by first deriving a model of individual clouds' phase $\varphi_n$, then making a model of the phase distribution function $\rho$, and finally using $\rho$ to obtain an analytical solution of the ensemble-averaged quantity $\widetilde{\Theta}$, which approximately obeys a harmonic oscillator.
elements.sent-2411-289: The harmonic oscillator approximation is valid under four assumptions: i) an equal time duration of the shallow and deep stage, ii) a small frequency difference between the wave and convection, iii) a weak synchronization status, and iv) a weak wave forcing.
elements.sent-2411-290: When the convection and wave frequency match, the ensemble quantity $\widetilde{\Theta}$ exhibits a resonant growth.
elements.sent-2411-291: The analytical solution reproduces the in-phase relation between $\widetilde{\Theta}$ and $\widetilde{W}$ seen in the cloud-permitting simulation.
elements.sent-2411-292: The key findings within the microscopic model are summarized below:.
elements.sent-2411-293: The dynamics are nonlinear for individual clouds but are approximately linear for the cloud ensemble.
elements.sent-2411-294: The phase modulation of individual clouds appears as the amplitude modulation of the cloud ensemble.
elements.sent-2411-295: The restoring force of the cloud ensemble oscillation is provided by the shallow-to-deep and deep-to-shallow stage transition.
elements.sent-2411-296: The synchronization is essentially the resonance of the cloud ensemble with the external forcing.
elements.sent-2411-297: The microscopic model suggests that synchronization is a potential way for a cloud ensemble to respond to an oscillatory forcing, when the wave period is close to the convective lifecycle.
elements.sent-2411-298: We hope the theory of synchronization and the algorithm for diagnosing synchronization can advance the research of tropical convection and be applied to improve convective parameterization schemes.

elements.sent-2411-369: When the member size of the cloud ensemble is sufficiently large, we can define a phase distribution function $\rho(\varphi,t)$, which describes the probability density of convection at a given phase $\varphi$.
elements.sent-2411-370: Because the number of convective cells in the cloud ensemble is assumed conservative, $\rho$ obeys a conservation equation: \begin{equation}\label{eq:rho_general} \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial}{\partial \varphi} \left( \rho\frac{d\varphi}{dt} \right) = 0,\end{equation} which is essentially a Liouville equation that describes the evolution of a distribution function in the phase space \cite[e.g.,][]{yano2017convective .}

(\ref{eq:rho_general ), we get a closed $\rho$ equation: \begin{equation}\label{eq:rho_expanded} \begin{split} \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = - \frac{\partial}{\partial \varphi} \left[ \rho \omega - \rho K \sin \left( \varphi - \varphi_w \right) \right] = - \omega \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial \varphi} + K \sin (\varphi - \varphi_w) \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial \varphi} + \rho K \cos \left( \varphi - \varphi_w \right). \end{split}\end{equation} Assuming the synchronization status is weak (the shape of $\rho$ is relatively uniform with $\varphi$) and the synchronization rate is slow (the wave forcing is weak): \begin{equation}\left| \rho - \frac{1}{2\pi} \right| \ll \frac{1}{2\pi}, \quad K \ll \omega,\end{equation} the second term on the right-hand side of Eq. (\ref{eq:rho_expanded ) can be neglected, so the $\rho$ equation is approximated as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:linearized_rho_equation} \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \omega \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial \varphi} \approx \frac{K}{2\pi} \cos \left( \varphi - \varphi_w \right) = \frac{K}{2\pi} \cos \left( \varphi - \check{\varphi}_w - \Omega t \right).\end{equation}.}}}
